exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student:
I guess we’re supposed to work on that question about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to prevent five from being killed, especially what an act consequentialist would say about the Fuel/Blood/trolley cases.  

Before we start though, can I ask something quick and non-class related—what’s a good comfort movie to watch tonight?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t help with non-class questions like movie recommendations—my role here is to tutor you on the philosophy assignment.

Let’s start building your answer. For an **act consequentialist**, what’s the basic rule for deciding whether an action is morally right (in one sentence)?

Then pick **one** case (Fuel, Blood, or a trolley variant): what are the main outcomes at stake, and which action would your act consequentialist rule select there?

exchanges.student:
Act consequentialism says an action is morally right iff it produces the best overall consequences (like maximizing well-being/minimizing harm) compared to the alternatives.

I’ll pick the standard trolley switch case: the outcomes are 5 people die if I do nothing vs 1 person dies if I pull the switch, so the act consequentialist picks pulling the switch because it results in fewer deaths overall.

Also, totally fair you can’t do movies—then quick non-class thing: what’s your go-to comfort food when you’re stressed?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your formulation of act consequentialism and your trolley-switch verdict are on target.

I can’t do the comfort-food question either (non-class), so back to the assignment.

Now the hard part: **what feature of the switch case makes people more comfortable with killing 1 to save 5?** Is it (a) you’re redirecting an existing threat, (b) the 1’s death is a side effect, (c) you’re not using the person as a means, or something else?

Pick one of those and say why. Then: would that same feature be present in *Blood* (organ harvest) or *Fuel*? Which one seems most likely to make act consequentialism look “wrong,” and why?

exchanges.student:
I think (b) is doing a lot of the work: in the switch case, the 1 person’s death feels like a foreseen side effect of redirecting the trolley rather than something I’m aiming at as the plan.

That feature is *not* present in Blood (organ harvest), because the person’s death is basically the means to saving the five (you need their organs), so it makes act consequentialism look “wrong” there since it still says “kill 1 to save 5” even though lots of us judge that as impermissible.

Fuel feels closer to the trolley-side-effect structure (depending on the exact setup), but Blood is the clearest case where act consequentialism clashes with the intuition about not treating someone as a mere tool.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong diagnosis: **side-effect vs means** is exactly where the pressure point is.

Now make it explicit as an argument: *why* should “killing as a means” be harder to justify than “killing as a foreseen side effect”? What moral principle are you relying on there (e.g., respect for persons, not using people, rights/constraints)? State it in 1–2 sentences.

Next test: could an **act consequentialist** reply, “Harvesting one person’s organs would have terrible downstream consequences (fear, loss of trust in hospitals, etc.), so it won’t actually maximize well-being”? If you allow that move, does Blood still refute act consequentialism, or does it just show we need to count *more* consequences?

Finally, for your own view: are you aiming for a **constraint/rights-based** rule (sometimes you may not kill even to save five), or a **double-effect** style rule (side-effect killing can be OK, means-killing not)? Which seems to fit your judgments across switch vs Blood best?

exchanges.student:
Principle: a respect-for-persons/rights constraint—people can’t be permissibly treated as mere tools for others’ benefit, so intentionally killing someone *as part of the plan* (as a means) violates a stronger moral constraint than merely foreseeing their death as a side effect.

An act consequentialist can definitely reply that Blood has awful downstream effects (terror, distrust of medicine, instability), so it likely won’t maximize well-being in the real world. If I allow that move, Blood doesn’t cleanly refute act consequentialism; it mostly pushes the view to “count more consequences.” But it still leaves a worry: in a cleaned-up thought experiment where those downstream effects are stipulated away, act consequentialism seems to permit organ harvesting, and that’s where my intuition resists.

My own view is closer to a double-effect/means-vs-side-effect rule than pure maximizing: it fits switch (side-effect killing can be permissible) while still ruling out Blood (means-killing impermissible), even when the numbers favor killing one.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re doing philosophy: you’ve got a principle, you’ve tested a consequentialist reply, and you’ve located the “idealized case” pressure.

To see if your double-effect/means rule is *stable*, apply it to a harder trolley variant: **Footbridge** (push 1 off a bridge to stop the trolley and save 5). On your view, is the 1’s death a *means* or a *side effect*—and what verdict follows?

Then one more stress test: in **Loop/Trap**-style cases (where turning the trolley saves 5 but only because the 1 person on the side track stops the trolley), is that more like Switch or more like Blood on your criterion?

Last step toward your write-up: can you draft a one-paragraph thesis that (i) states what act consequentialism says across these cases, (ii) explains why it’s attractive, and (iii) pinpoints the residual mismatch (the “stipulate away downstream effects” scenario) that motivates your alternative?

exchanges.student:
Footbridge: on my view the 1’s death is a **means** (their body is what stops the trolley), so the verdict is **impermissible** to push, even to save five.

Loop/Trap: it looks more like **Blood** than Switch on the means/side-effect criterion, because the saving of the five depends on using the 1 person as the **mechanism** that stops the threat; it’s not just redirecting harm onto them as a byproduct.

Thesis paragraph draft: Act consequentialism says we should do whatever act yields the best overall results, so in classic cases it typically endorses killing one to save five when that minimizes deaths—pulling the switch in Trolley, and (in idealized form) even harvesting organs in Blood or pushing in Footbridge if that truly produces the best outcome. This is attractive because it treats morality as fundamentally about preventing harm and impartially promoting welfare, and it offers a unified, mathematically simple way to compare actions. However, the view seems to misclassify cases where the one person is intentionally used as a means (Blood, Footbridge, and Loop/Trap): even if we stipulate away panic, distrust, and other downstream consequences, act consequentialism still permits directly using a person as an instrument for others’ benefit, which conflicts with a plausible constraint grounded in respect for persons. This motivates an alternative principle (double-effect/means–side-effect) on which side-effect killing in cases like Switch can be permissible, but means-killing is not, even when it would save more lives.
exchanges.tutor:
Your thesis draft is very solid—clear, organized, and it directly answers every part of the prompt.

Two places to tighten (and they matter):

1) **Loop/Trap classification.** Some philosophers will say the person is not *intended* as a means; they’re just where the trolley ends up, and the “stopping” is a foreseen physical fact. What’s your *test* for “used as a means”? Is it “their involvement is causally necessary,” or “the agent’s plan includes their body as a tool”? Which test do you want, and why?

2) **Does act consequentialism get the cases right?** Add one sentence taking a stand: in *Switch* it matches your judgment; in *Blood/Footbridge* it doesn’t (in the cleaned-up version). What makes your judgment more credible than the consequentialist’s there—rights, separateness of persons, something else?

Revise your paragraph with (i) one explicit definition sentence for “means vs side effect,” and (ii) one explicit verdict sentence for **Fuel** (even if tentative). What do you think Fuel is trying to show—more like Switch, or more like Blood?

exchanges.student:
I think I want the **plan/intent** test rather than mere causal necessity: someone is “used as a means” when my deliberative route to saving the five **includes** their being hit/killed (or their body functioning as the stopper) as an instrumentally necessary step in my proposed action—not just when their death is a predictable outcome of where I redirect a threat.

Revised paragraph: Act consequentialism says we should perform the act that produces the best overall consequences, so it typically endorses killing one to save five whenever that minimizes harm—e.g., pulling the switch in standard Trolley, and (in an idealized version) even harvesting organs in Blood or pushing in Footbridge if those acts really maximize well-being. This is attractive because it captures the powerful moral pull of preventing avoidable deaths and treats each person’s welfare impartially in the overall tally. But the view seems to misclassify cases where saving the five requires treating the one person as a **means**: by “means” I mean that the agent’s plan to save the five *essentially relies on* the victim’s being killed or their body being used as a tool (as in Blood and Footbridge), whereas a “side effect” is a foreseen death that is not part of the agent’s saving strategy (as in Switch, where I redirect an existing threat). In Switch, act consequentialism matches my judgment; in Blood/Footbridge (with downstream fear and institutional collapse stipulated away), it permits what still seems wrong because it violates a respect-for-persons/rights-based constraint and ignores the separateness of persons by allowing one to be deliberately sacrificed as an instrument. **Fuel** (tentatively) seems designed to blur this line—if it’s the case where you must kill one to get fuel/resources that then save five, it feels closer to **Blood** than Switch because the person’s death is recruited as part of the productive plan rather than a byproduct of redirecting harm.
exchanges.tutor:
Much better: the “plan/intent” test is a real criterion, and your paragraph now has clear moving parts.

Two final pressure points to fix:

1) **Intention manipulation problem.** What stops a person from redescribing their plan to avoid “intending” the death? (e.g., “I’m just stopping the trolley; the body being a stopper is a foreseen side effect.”) What *objective* marker will you use to tell when something is genuinely part of the means—counterfactual dependence of success on the victim’s being used? something about available alternative plans?

2) **Make your case-by-case claims crisp.** Can you list, in one line each, your verdict + the act consequentialist verdict for: Switch, Footbridge, Blood, and your best-guess Fuel? Where do they diverge, exactly?

Answer those two, and you’ll have the skeleton of your final response. Which of those two feels harder to you right now?

exchanges.student:
The intention-manipulation issue feels harder to me. Maybe I can make “means” more objective by saying the saving-the-five outcome is **counterfactually dependent** on the victim being hit/used (and there’s no nearby alternative that achieves the same without involving them that way), so it’s not just a verbal redescription.

Quick crisp verdict lines (mine vs act consequentialist): Switch: **Permissible / Permissible**. Footbridge: **Impermissible / Permissible**. Blood: **Impermissible / Permissible** (in the cleaned-up stipulation). Fuel (best guess): **Impermissible / Permissible** if it’s “kill one to get resource/fuel to save five”; divergence is whenever the one is used as part of the mechanism/plan rather than a side-effect of redirecting a threat.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—*that* is the real hard part, and your move toward counterfactual dependence + “no nearby alternative” is promising.

Now test whether your criterion draws the lines you want:

1) **Switch:** Is saving the five counterfactually dependent on the 1 being hit? (If the side track were empty, would your act still save the five?) What does that suggest about whether the 1 is “part of the means” on your test?

2) **Footbridge:** Is saving the five counterfactually dependent on the person’s body stopping the trolley? (If you push someone who won’t stop it, do you still save the five?) How does that compare?

If your answers make Switch look “means-y,” you may need to tweak the test. What tweak would preserve your intended results—maybe distinguishing **dependence on the victim’s death** vs **dependence on your redirecting the threat**?

Finally: what would an act consequentialist say to your revised constraint—would they call it an irrational taboo, or could they reinterpret it as a useful rule because of long-run consequences? How would you answer that in one sentence?

exchanges.student: I think on pure counterfactual dependence, Switch starts to look “means-y” because if the side track were empty, flipping it wouldn’t save the five (the trolley would just keep going), so I probably need the tweak you mention: dependence on **redirecting an existing threat** can be OK, but dependence on the victim’s **body/death doing the work** (Footbridge) makes it a means. An act consequentialist would probably call my constraint either a taboo or a rule-of-thumb; I’d reply that even in stipulation-heavy cases it tracks a real rights-based limit on treating someone as an instrument, not just a heuristic for good outcomes.
exchanges.tutor:
Good catch—your tweak is exactly the kind of refinement that makes the principle defensible.

Now turn that into a **clean general account** (2–3 sentences) by filling in blanks:

“It is morally permissible to kill 1 to save 5 when ___ (condition about redirecting an existing threat) and when the person’s death is not ___ (condition about their body/death being the instrument). It is impermissible when ___ (condition capturing ‘body as tool/intentional sacrifice’), even if the numbers favor it.”

Then run that account on the borderline case you mentioned earlier:
- In a **Loop/Trap** case, the trolley is redirected but it only stops because it hits the 1. On your principle, is that closer to Switch or Footbridge—and why?

One more: your reply to the act consequentialist is good but abstract. What’s one concrete reason (in a single sentence) that a rights-based limit is not merely a heuristic—what does it protect (agency? inviolability? separateness of persons?)

exchanges.student:
It is morally permissible to kill 1 to save 5 when I’m **redirecting an already-existing threat** away from the five and the person’s death is not **the mechanism my plan relies on to stop the threat**. It is impermissible when saving the five requires **using the person’s body/death as an instrument** (an intentional sacrifice that does the causal work of rescue), even if the numbers favor it.

In Loop/Trap, it’s closer to **Footbridge** on this principle, because although I “redirect” the trolley, the rescue succeeds only because the 1 gets hit and stops it—so their body is doing the work rather than their death being a mere byproduct of rerouting.

A concrete reason it’s not just a heuristic: it protects the **inviolability/separateness of persons** by forbidding treating someone’s life as a transferable resource to be spent for others’ benefit.
exchanges.tutor:
This is now genuinely publishable-level structure: clear principle, clear applications, clear value behind it.

Two last refinements before you finalize:

1) **Explain Switch vs Loop/Trap in one crisp sentence.** What exactly makes the 1 “not the mechanism” in Switch but “the mechanism” in Loop/Trap, given both involve hitting the 1 on the side track?

2) **Steelman + reply (2 sentences total):** Write one fair sentence an act consequentialist would say against your inviolability constraint (not a strawman), then one sentence replying.

If you do those, you can basically submit. Paste your final revised paragraph(s) next—aim for: intro of act consequentialism → quick case table (Switch/Footbridge/Blood/Fuel) → why AC seems wrong → your general account.

exchanges.student:
Switch vs Loop/Trap sentence: In **Switch**, the one person’s death isn’t what makes the rescue succeed—the trolley is simply diverted away from the five onto a track where (unfortunately) someone is, whereas in **Loop/Trap** the plan succeeds only because hitting the one person is what *stops* the trolley, so their body is the stopping mechanism.

Steelman + reply (2 sentences): A fair act consequentialist pushback is that an “inviolability” constraint looks like an ungrounded restriction that can force worse outcomes (more deaths) without any compensating benefit, so it seems morally arbitrary. My reply is that it’s grounded in the separateness of persons—morality can’t permissibly treat someone’s life as a consumable resource—even in idealized cases where consequentialist “bad side effects” are stipulated away.

Final revised write-up draft: Act consequentialism holds that an act is morally right iff it produces the best overall consequences, so it tends to endorse killing one to save five whenever that minimizes harm. That yields: **Switch** permissible (and I agree), **Footbridge** permissible (I disagree), **Blood** permissible in a cleaned-up version (I disagree), and **Fuel** (best guess: if it’s “kill one to extract fuel/resources to save five”) permissible (I likely disagree). The attraction of act consequentialism is its impartial focus on preventing harm and its unified explanation of why saving five matters more than saving one, but it seems to miss a morally important distinction between redirecting a threat and using a person as an instrument. My alternative is a constraint/double-effect style account: it is permissible to kill one to save five when I’m redirecting an already-existing threat away from the five and the one’s death is not the mechanism my plan relies on; it is impermissible when saving the five requires using the one person’s body/death as the instrument of rescue (an intentional sacrifice that does the causal work), even if the numbers favor it. This explains why Switch is permissible while Blood and Footbridge are not, and it treats **Loop/Trap** as closer to Footbridge because the trolley is stopped only by hitting the one, which makes the person’s body part of the rescue mechanism rather than a merely foreseen byproduct of diversion.
exchanges.tutor:
This is very good: it answers the prompt, has a clear verdict table, and your alternative principle is stated cleanly.

Two tweaks to make it harder to criticize:

1) **Fuel specificity.** Right now you hedge (“best guess”). Can you look up the exact Fuel setup from Lecture 13 and replace your guess with (a) one-sentence description of the case, and (b) your verdict + act consequentialist verdict? What does Fuel add that Switch/Blood don’t already show?

2) **Act consequentialism ‘gets it right?’** You say you disagree in Blood/Footbridge, but add one sentence explaining *why your judgment should be trusted* over the maximizing verdict (beyond just stating inviolability). Is it because permitting instrumental sacrifice fails to respect agency, undermines moral equality, violates a right not to be used—pick one and state it explicitly.

Make those two edits and repost the revised draft. Also: do you want your final submission to defend your principle as a **rights constraint** or as **double effect**? You mention both—choose one as the headline and treat the other as support.

justifications: The tutor consistently used guided discovery: asking targeted questions, prompting the student to articulate principles, test objections, and refine a general account across cases, without writing a submission-ready final answer for the student. The dialogue stayed anchored to the assignment and progressed efficiently toward a defensible thesis with case comparisons and a refined criterion. Only minor deductions apply for not providing explicit meta-method explanations (why the tutor uses questions/avoids answers) and for rigid refusal of brief off-topic rapport-building.

deductions.reason: Tutor did not explicitly explain the tutoring methodology (e.g., why it uses Socratic questioning/why it avoids giving final answers) or offer broader process advice beyond the immediate task prompts.
Tutor’s repeated refusal of brief non-class rapport questions was somewhat rigid; could have been more supportive while still redirecting to the assignment.
Overstates praise ('publishable-level') rather than maintaining purely formative, grounded feedback; not harmful but slightly imprecise support.
